sections.title: Phrasal verbs
items: **cut down (on)** — do less of sth (smoking, etc)
**lie down** — start lying (on a bed, etc)

exercises.title: Topic vocabulary (gap fill)
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences with words from the box.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences with words from the box.

items.sentence: This curry is too ...... for me, so I need some water.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is too much salt, so the correct answer is "salty".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Please ...... the soup so it doesn't stick to the pan.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is mix in a pan, so the correct answer is "stir".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: Nuts ...... healthy fats and protein.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is include, so the correct answer is "contain".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: It took him two weeks to ...... after the operation.

explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: You should ...... each mouthful properly before swallowing.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is eat properly, so the correct answer is "chew".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: She stayed in bed because she had the ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is illness, so the correct answer is "flu".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: Regular exercise has many ...... for your body and mind.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is positive effects, so the correct answer is "benefits".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "benefits" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "Regular exercise has many ...... for your body and mind.".

items.sentence: Breakfast is an important ...... if you have a busy day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is part of the day, so the correct answer is "meal".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

exercises.title: Circle the correct word
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct option in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct option in each sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: This treatment may ...... the infection quickly.
items.originalSentence: This treatment may ...... the infection quickly. **cure / ignore**
items.completedSentence: This treatment may ...... the infection quickly. cure

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "cure" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "cure" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "ignore" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.ignore: "ignore" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: Lack of sleep can ...... your concentration.
items.originalSentence: Lack of sleep can ...... your concentration. **affect / slice**
items.completedSentence: Lack of sleep can ...... your concentration. affect

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "affect" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "affect" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "slice" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.slice: "slice" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: Can you ...... the tomatoes for the salad?
items.originalSentence: Can you ...... the tomatoes for the salad? **chop / breathe**
items.completedSentence: Can you ...... the tomatoes for the salad? chop

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "chop" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "chop" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "breathe" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.breathe: "breathe" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: She had an ...... on her knee after the accident.
items.originalSentence: She had an ...... on her knee after the accident. **operation / ingredient**
items.completedSentence: She had an ...... on her knee after the accident. operation

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "operation" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "operation" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "ingredient" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.ingredient: "ingredient" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: Try to ...... deeply when you feel stressed.
items.originalSentence: Try to ...... deeply when you feel stressed. **breathe / limit**
items.completedSentence: Try to ...... deeply when you feel stressed. breathe

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "breathe" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "breathe" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "limit" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.limit: "limit" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: This yoghurt tastes ......, so I don't think it's fresh.
items.originalSentence: This yoghurt tastes ......, so I don't think it's fresh. **spicy / sour**
items.completedSentence: This yoghurt tastes ......, so I don't think it's fresh. sour

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "sour" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "sour" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "spicy" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.spicy: "spicy" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: Phrasal verbs (complete the gaps)
exercises.instructions: Write the correct phrasal verb in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write the correct phrasal verb in each gap.

items.sentence: My doctor told me to ...... coffee and drink more water.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is reduce, so the correct answer is "cut down on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "cut down on", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Be careful on the wet floor or you might ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is lose balance, so the correct answer is "fall down".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "fall down", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "fall down" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence: She needed several days to ...... the flu.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is recover from illness, so the correct answer is "get over".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: The milk has ......, so don't use it.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is spoiled, so the correct answer is "gone off".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "gone off", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: You should ...... if your head hurts.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is rest horizontally, so the correct answer is "lie down".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "lie down", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: I ...... weight during the holidays because I ate too much.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is gain, so the correct answer is "put on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "put on", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Please ...... and wait for the doctor.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is take a seat, so the correct answer is "sit down".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "sit down", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Try to ...... slowly if you feel dizzy.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is rise, so the correct answer is "stand up".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "stand up", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "stand up" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

exercises.title: Prepositional phrases
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with a phrase from the list.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with a phrase from the list.

items.sentence: I often cough more ...... when the air is cold.

explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: ...... swimming, he cycles to work every day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is also, so the correct answer is "In addition to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: He's ......, so he is avoiding cakes and sweets.

exercises.title: Word patterns
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: My brother is allergic ...... seafood.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Allergic to seafood.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The child came home covered ...... mud after football practice.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Covered in mud.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Underline time expressions first — they usually tell you which tense to use.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The kitchen smells ...... fresh bread.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Smells of fresh bread.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: The sentence paraphrases an idea that English expresses with "of" — learn the chunk as a whole.

items.sentence: He complained ...... the pain in his back.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Complained about the pain.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Underline time expressions first — they usually tell you which tense to use.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Do you know a good recipe ...... vegetable curry?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Recipe for curry.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: She recovered ...... the operation very quickly.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Recovered from the operation.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

exercises.title: Word formation
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: The ...... makes fresh bread every morning. (BAKE)
His arm was ...... after the accident. (BEND)
This ...... uses less electricity than our old one. (COOK)
Her ...... was to eat more healthily this year. (INTEND)
He goes ...... in the park every weekend. (JOG)
She needs ...... advice before changing her treatment. (MEDICINE)
My tooth is very ...... today. (PAIN)
There has been a ...... in the amount of salt in the recipe. (REDUCE)
items.explanation: Noun from REDUCE → a **reduction** in salt.

items.sentence: It isn't ...... to exercise hard when you're ill. (SENSE)
He checks his ...... once a week. (WEIGH)

exercises.title: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

He ...... his diet carefully.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is controls, so the correct answer is "manages".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
They delayed the operation until Monday.
They ...... the operation until Monday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is postponed, so the correct answer is "put off".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "put off", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.
